title: UCLA vs Duke — Women’s Elite Eight Viewing Guide
eventTag: NCAA Women’s Tournament • Elite Eight • Sacramento, CA
venue: Golden 1 Center, Sacramento, California • ABC / ESPN
extraInfo: Winner advances to the Final Four in Tampa, FL (April 4-6)

stakes.callout: A trip to the Final Four is on the line. No. 1 UCLA — 34-1 with the nation's No. 1 offense — faces a Duke team riding the magic of Ashlon Jackson's buzzer-beater over LSU. These teams met on Thanksgiving and UCLA won by 30. But that was without Lauren Betts. Duke hasn't faced the 6-7 All-American center yet. Six UCLA seniors are playing their final games before a potentially historic WNBA Draft. For Duke, reaching the Elite Eight was the surprise — now they have nothing to lose. This is the best offense in the country vs. the team that just produced the Shot of the Tournament.

cards.label: Six Seniors, One Last Run
cards.value: Lauren Betts, Kiki Rice, Gabriela Jaquez, Gianna Kneepkens, Charlisse Leger-Walker, and Angela Dugalić are all playing their final college games. Up to six could be drafted in the 2026 WNBA Draft — which would break the all-time record for most players drafted from one school in a single year. But first: unfinished business. Last year's Final Four ended in an 85-51 loss to UConn. This time, they want to go further.

cards.label: The Betts Factor
cards.value: Lauren Betts didn't play in the November meeting, when UCLA still won 89-59. Now she's back — averaging 17.1 PPG, 8.6 RPG, and 2.9 BPG with 64.8% shooting. She's the Big Ten Player of the Year AND Defensive Player of the Year. Duke has no answer for a 6-7 center shooting nearly 65% from the field with elite rim protection. Her career-high 35 against Oklahoma State showed she's peaking at the right time.

cards.label: Duke's Buzzer-Beater Magic
cards.value: Ashlon Jackson's corner three at the buzzer — spinning around the rim before falling — gave Duke an 87-85 win over LSU in one of the best Sweet 16 games in women's tournament history. Jackson had missed two free throws moments before, making the redemption even sweeter. Duke is playing with house money and a belief that borders on destiny. That's dangerous.

cards.label: Two Coaching Legends
cards.value: Cori Close is a 15-year UCLA coach who swept all four national Coach of the Year awards last season. Kara Lawson is an Olympic gold medalist, WNBA champion, and former NBA assistant coach who played for Pat Summitt at Tennessee. Both have built their programs from the ground up. Close's philosophy: "Sometimes you. Sometimes me. Always us." Lawson's edge: she's been an underdog before.

styles.title: No. 1 Offense in America, Elite Balance
bullets: Four double-figure scorers: Betts (17.1), Rice (15.2), Jaquez (14.1), Kneepkens (13.2). UCLA's 68.1% assist rate is extraordinary — nearly seven of every ten made baskets are assisted. You can't key on one player because all four will hurt you.

No. 1 offensive efficiency nationally (122.7): UCLA scores 85.3 PPG on 51.3% shooting with a .571 eFG%. They also shoot 37.2% from three and 77.7% from the line. The Bruins turn every possession into a high-quality look.

Lauren Betts dominates the paint: At 6-7, she shoots 64.8% from the field and blocks 2.9 shots per game (5th nationally). She scored 35 on 15-of-19 shooting in the last round. Duke's tallest player, Fournier (6-2), gives up five inches to Betts.

Full-court press creates chaos: UCLA's press sparked an 11-0 run against Cal Baptist and has been a weapon all tournament. Against Duke's three-guard lineup, the press could force turnovers and create easy transition buckets.

One weakness — turnovers against pressure: The lone loss (Texas, 76-65) came when UCLA committed 20 turnovers against aggressive defense. Duke's 85 steals from Mair and Jackson's active hands could disrupt UCLA's flow — if they can get into passing lanes.

styles.title: Defense-First Identity, Buzzer-Beater Belief
bullets: Suffocating defense is the foundation: Duke allows 59.4 PPG with Lawson's aggressive switching man-to-man. Taina Mair leads with 85 steals, and the team has shown the ability to completely neutralize opponents (held Baylor to 46, Charleston to 64). The challenge: UCLA scores 85.3.

Three-headed backcourt attack: Jackson (11.5 PPG, 73 threes), Mair (11.4 PPG, 5.6 APG, ACC Tourney MVP), and freshman Skinner give Duke backcourt depth that few teams match. Against LSU, both Mair and Fournier scored 22 while Jackson added the game-winner.

Toby Fournier is a two-way force: The 6-2 sophomore averages 17.5 PPG on 52.7% shooting with 78 blocks. She's Duke's best player and the key matchup against UCLA's frontcourt. But her 58.1% free throw shooting is a vulnerability UCLA can exploit.

Offensive evolution proved against LSU: Duke scored 87 points against LSU, their highest output in the tournament. The Blue Devils are no longer just a grind-it-out defensive team — they've shown they can shoot with anyone. But consistency remains a question.

Nothing to lose: Duke already accomplished more than expected. Jackson's buzzer-beater created a narrative of destiny. The Blue Devils have film from the 89-59 Thanksgiving loss and know exactly what went wrong. Lawson's teams improve through the season. That said, UCLA was without Betts in that game — she makes them even better.

keys.title: Lauren Betts is the matchup Duke can't solve.
keys.description: Betts didn't play when these teams met in November, and UCLA still won by 30. Now Duke faces a 6-7 center shooting 64.8% with 2.9 blocks per game — the Big Ten's Player AND Defensive Player of the Year. Toby Fournier (6-2) is Duke's best interior defender, but she gives up five inches. If Betts gets deep post position, Duke has no answer. She scored 35 on 15-of-19 against Oklahoma State and 16 on 7-of-9 in the Sweet 16. Duke must double early, contest every catch, and live with the consequences of helping off UCLA's shooters.

keys.title: Can Duke's defense survive UCLA's four-headed attack?
keys.description: UCLA has four players averaging 13+ PPG — and a 68.1% assist rate. Duke's defense allows 59.4 PPG, but no team they've faced this tournament scores 85 per game. In November, UCLA scored 30 points in the first quarter alone. Duke's switching scheme needs to be perfect: contain Betts in the post, track Kneepkens (37.2% from three) off screens, stay attached to Rice (15.2 PPG, 90.3% FT), and limit Jaquez's drives. Miss one assignment and UCLA makes you pay.

keys.title: Turnovers are Duke's path to an upset.
keys.description: UCLA's lone loss came when Texas forced 20 turnovers and led by 23 in the first half. Duke's backcourt is built for disruption — Mair has 85 steals and Jackson's active hands created chaos against LSU. If Duke can force UCLA into 16+ turnovers, it shortens possessions and negates the Bruins' half-court efficiency. UCLA's press can be turned against them too: if Duke handles pressure and pushes tempo, they can create transition looks before UCLA's defense sets.

keys.title: The free throw line could decide it.
keys.description: UCLA shoots 77.7% from the line. But Fournier (58.1%) and Betts (whose FT% has been a career weakness) could be targets for intentional fouling in crunch time. Meanwhile, Kiki Rice shoots 90.3% — she went 6-for-6 against Minnesota and 10-for-10 against Cal Baptist. If this game is close in the fourth quarter, who's at the line matters enormously. Duke's strategy may involve fouling Betts early and hoping Rice doesn't get there.

keys.title: Emotional momentum: destiny vs. dominance.
keys.description: Duke is riding the high of one of the most iconic buzzer-beaters in tournament history. That creates belief — a dangerous commodity in March. But UCLA has been on a mission since last year's 85-51 Final Four loss to UConn. Six seniors playing their last college games, a potential record-breaking WNBA Draft class, and the program's first-ever NCAA title on the horizon. The Bruins have the better team on paper by a wide margin. The question is whether Duke's emotional momentum can overcome UCLA's talent advantage. Close's message to her team: "It's all or nothing for us."

prediction.summary: UCLA is the better team by virtually every statistical measure — No. 1 offense nationally, four double-figure scorers, the nation's most dominant center, and 34-1 record. Duke's defense is real, and the buzzer-beater over LSU creates genuine belief, but the Blue Devils haven't faced anyone like UCLA this tournament. In November, UCLA won 89-59 without Lauren Betts. With Betts, the size and efficiency advantages are overwhelming. Duke will compete — Lawson's teams always do — and the first quarter could be tight. But UCLA's depth, shooting, and Betts' interior dominance should take over in the second half. The Bruins advance to the Final Four to complete their seniors' redemption arc.

phases.title: First Quarter
phases.description: Duke comes out aggressive after the LSU emotional high, and Mair pushes tempo. Jackson hits an early three. But UCLA settles in quickly — Rice finds Betts for an easy bucket in the post, and Kneepkens drills a catch-and-shoot three. Betts blocks a Fournier attempt on the other end. UCLA leads 22-17 after one, with Duke hanging around on adrenaline.

phases.title: Second Quarter
phases.description: UCLA's press starts to create problems. Dugalić comes off the bench and hits two midrange jumpers. Duke commits three turnovers in a two-minute stretch as Mair struggles against UCLA's length. Fournier keeps Duke in it with 8 second-quarter points, but Betts is a problem inside — she has 12 first-half points on 6-of-8 shooting. UCLA leads 42-31 at the break.

phases.title: Third Quarter
phases.description: This is where UCLA pulls away. The Bruins open the half on a 10-2 run — the same third-quarter dominance they showed against Minnesota (held to 12 points) and Cal Baptist (11-0 run). Jaquez hits a three, Rice converts a transition layup, and Betts swats another shot. Duke calls timeout down 18. Jackson answers with a difficult pull-up jumper, but Kneepkens responds immediately. The deficit is 20 entering the fourth.

phases.title: Fourth Quarter
phases.description: Duke makes one last push — Fournier scores on consecutive possessions and Mair steals an entry pass for a fast break. The lead shrinks to 15. But Rice calmly hits four free throws (she shoots 90.3%), and Betts finishes with a hook shot that effectively ends it. The UCLA bench celebrates as the final minutes tick away. Six seniors are going to the Final Four.

projectedScore.rationale: UCLA's size advantage (Betts at 6-7 vs. Fournier at 6-2), superior shooting (51.3% vs. 44.0%), and four-headed scoring attack are too much for Duke to overcome. The Bruins have dominated the third quarter all tournament, and their depth wears opponents down. Duke's defense will keep it closer than the 30-point November margin, but UCLA's talent wins comfortably.

players.name: Lauren Betts
players.meta: C • 6-7 • Senior • Big Ten Player & Defensive Player of the Year • Wade Trophy Finalist
players.bio:
Born in Vitoria-Gasteiz, Spain, to a professional basketball father (Andrew, drafted by Charlotte) and a national champion volleyball mother (Michelle, Long Beach State '93). Younger sister Sienna is a freshman teammate. The No. 1 recruit in 2022, she transferred from Stanford and became the first player in Big Ten history to win Player of the Year and Defensive Player of the Year simultaneously. Scored a career-high 35 on 15-of-19 shooting in her Pauley Pavilion farewell vs. Oklahoma State. Projected top-4 pick in the 2026 WNBA Draft. Duke has never faced her — she sat out the November meeting.
Read more →

players.name: Kiki Rice
players.meta: G • 5-11 • Senior • AP Third Team All-American • Big Ten Tournament MVP
players.bio:
Raised in Washington, D.C. (attended Sidwell Friends School). Her aunt is diplomat Susan Rice; she's a cousin of former NBA player Allan Houston. Father and brother both played basketball at Yale. The highest-ranked recruit in UCLA history, Rice chose the Bruins over UConn, Stanford, and Duke. Led all scorers with 21 points in the Sweet 16 win over Minnesota. Shoots 90.3% from the free throw line. Projected top-10 pick in the 2026 WNBA Draft.
Read more →

players.name: Gianna Kneepkens
players.meta: G • 6-0 • Graduate • Transfer from Utah • Pac-12 Freshman of the Year (2022)
players.bio:
Youngest of six siblings from Duluth, Minnesota. Grew up playing against five older brothers. Scored a Minnesota state-record 43.1 PPG as a high school senior, including a 67-point game (state all-time record). After four years at Utah, she chose UCLA over South Carolina and Texas. On pace for a 50/40/90 shooting season, she added 15 points in the Round of 32 win over Oklahoma State. A projected WNBA first-round pick whose shooting translates perfectly to the next level.
Read more →

players.name: Gabriela Jaquez
players.meta: F • 6-0 • Senior • All-Big Ten First Team • Sister of NBA's Jaime Jaquez Jr.
players.bio:
From Camarillo, California. Her brother Jaime Jaquez Jr. is a Miami Heat guard and the first Mexican-American first-round NBA draft pick. Gabriela co-won McDonald's All-American MVP with Rice. Her senior season is a breakout: career-high 54.6% FG and 40.2% from three (up from 34.8%). She's listed as a guard but plays every position. Quote: "We understand that we're super close this year. Each celebration means something more." Projected to be drafted in the 2026 WNBA Draft.
Read more →

players.name: Toby Fournier
players.meta: F • 6-2 • Sophomore • All-ACC First Team • ACC Rookie of the Year (2025)
players.bio:
Toronto native who attended Crestwood Prep in Ontario. Ranked 10th in ESPN's 2024 class. Won ACC Rookie of the Year as a freshman and has been even better as a sophomore. Scored 22 points against LSU in the Sweet 16 thriller and 24 in the first round. Her 78 blocks lead all ACC guards and forwards. The challenge: she gives up five inches to Lauren Betts (6-2 vs. 6-7) and shoots just 58.1% from the free throw line. Projected first-round WNBA pick in 2027.
Read more →

players.name: Ashlon Jackson
players.meta: G • 6-0 • Senior • Buzzer-Beater Hero vs. LSU
players.bio:
From China, Texas. Amassed 2,683 points, 705 assists, and 561 steals at Hardin-Jefferson High School. Hit the tournament's most iconic shot: a corner three that spun around the rim at the buzzer to beat LSU 87-85. She had missed two critical free throws moments before — making the redemption even more dramatic. A three-level scorer with a specialty mid-range pull-up, Jackson leads Duke with 73 made threes. Question: can she generate enough offense against UCLA's defense?
Read more →

players.name: Taina Mair
players.meta: G • 5-9 • Senior • ACC Tournament MVP • 9th in ACC history in assists
players.bio:
From Dorchester, Boston. Grew up in the community basketball program No Books No Ball at Curtis Hall Community Center. Transferred from Boston College and became Duke's engine. Ninth in ACC history with 649 career assists. Named ACC Tournament MVP after leading Duke to back-to-back conference titles. Scored 22 against LSU in the Sweet 16. Her 85 steals make her the most disruptive defender Duke has — she'll need to create turnovers to keep Duke in this game.
Read more →

players.name: Delaney Thomas
players.meta: F • 6-3 • Junior • Duke's Most Efficient Scorer
players.bio:
From Chicago, Illinois. Duke's most efficient scorer at a remarkable 61.5% from the field. Led all scorers with 17 points on 6-of-7 shooting in the 69-46 dismantling of Baylor. Drew a team-high 12 charges last season. Thomas brings physicality and interior toughness that Duke will need desperately against UCLA's frontcourt. At 6-3, she and Fournier (6-2) form Duke's interior rotation — both give up significant size to Betts and Dugalić.
Read more →

funFacts.title: UCLA Could Break a WNBA Draft Record
funFacts.body: If all six UCLA seniors are drafted, the Bruins would become the first school ever to have six players selected in one WNBA Draft. Four are projected first-rounders: Betts, Rice, Kneepkens, and Jaquez. The current record is five (Tennessee 1999, Notre Dame 2019, South Carolina 2023).

funFacts.title: The Spin Heard 'Round March
funFacts.body: Ashlon Jackson's buzzer-beater vs. LSU spun around the rim multiple times before dropping. Jackson had missed two free throws moments earlier. Mair's first attempt at a game-winner missed, but the ball went off LSU, giving Duke one more chance with 0.8 seconds left.

funFacts.title: UCLA's Original Championship (1978)
funFacts.body: UCLA won the 1978 AIAW National Championship — the predecessor to the NCAA Tournament. Wilt Chamberlain attended and told the Washington Post: "These girls can play. This is as good as watching the Lakers." Ann Meyers and Denise Curry's jerseys still hang in Pauley Pavilion.

funFacts.title: Kara Lawson: Gold Medalist, WNBA Champ, NBA Pioneer
funFacts.body: Duke's coach won an Olympic gold medal (2008 Beijing), a WNBA championship (Sacramento, 2005), was the first female NBA broadcast analyst (2007), and the first female coach in Boston Celtics history (2020). She played for Pat Summitt at Tennessee.

funFacts.title: The Betts Sisters
funFacts.body: Lauren (6-7, senior) and Sienna Betts (6-4, freshman) are teammates at UCLA. Their father Andrew played for Great Britain's national team and was drafted by the NBA's Charlotte Hornets. Their mother Michelle won a volleyball national championship at Long Beach State.

funFacts.title: From New Zealand to the Elite Eight
funFacts.body: UCLA's Charlisse Leger-Walker is of Samoan and Maori descent from New Zealand. Her mother competed in two Olympics. Charlisse debuted for New Zealand's senior national team at age 16, tore her ACL at UCLA, redshirted a full season, and is now competing in the Elite Eight.

funFacts.title: 67 Points in One High School Game
funFacts.body: UCLA's Gianna Kneepkens scored 67 points in a single high school game — the all-time Minnesota record. She averaged 43.1 PPG as a senior, also a state record. The youngest of six siblings, she grew up playing against five older brothers.

funFacts.title: Kiki Rice's Family Ties
funFacts.body: UCLA's Kiki Rice's aunt is diplomat Susan Rice (former U.S. National Security Advisor). She's a cousin of former NBA player Allan Houston. Her father and brother both played basketball at Yale, and her mother played tennis there.

funFacts.title: The Jaquez Family Legacy
funFacts.body: Gabriela Jaquez's brother Jaime Jaquez Jr. is a Miami Heat guard and the first Mexican-American first-round NBA draft pick. Both starred at Camarillo High and UCLA. Gabriela co-won McDonald's All-American MVP with her future teammate Kiki Rice.

funFacts.title: UCLA Beat Duke by 30... Without Their Best Player
funFacts.body: On Thanksgiving 2025, UCLA beat Duke 89-59 — outscoring them 30-7 in the first quarter. Lauren Betts didn't play. Now she's back, averaging 17.1 PPG on 64.8% shooting. Duke has never faced her.

funFacts.title: No Books No Ball
funFacts.body: Duke's Taina Mair grew up in Dorchester, Boston, playing through a community program called "No Books No Ball" at the Curtis Hall Community Center. She's now ninth in ACC history in career assists with 649.

funFacts.title: 34 Assists in One Game
funFacts.body: In the Big Ten Tournament Championship, UCLA set a tournament record with 34 assists in their 96-45 demolition of Iowa. The 51-point margin was the largest of any team over a top-10 opponent in NCAA history.

history.callout: UCLA and Duke have met just four times in women's basketball history, with the series tied 2-2. Their most recent meeting was a 89-59 UCLA blowout on Thanksgiving 2025. Before that, the programs hadn't played since the 2000-01 season. Now they meet in the Elite Eight with a Final Four trip on the line — the highest-stakes meeting in the rivalry's brief history.

timeline.text: Duke loses to UCLA, 83-93. The first meeting between the programs.
Duke wins 80-52. Blue Devils take the lead in the series.
UCLA blows out Duke 89-59 at the Players Era Championship. The Bruins scored 30 points in the first quarter. Lauren Betts did not play. Series tied 2-2.
Elite Eight — the first NCAA Tournament meeting between these programs. A Final Four trip is on the line.

subsections.title: UCLA's Path to the Final Four

timeline.text: UCLA wins the AIAW National Championship under Billie Moore. Ann Meyers leads the Bruins past Maryland, 90-74, in front of 9,531 fans at Pauley Pavilion. Wilt Chamberlain attends.
UCLA reaches the Elite Eight under Cori Close, losing to eventual runner-up Mississippi State.
UCLA reaches the Final Four for the first time in the NCAA era. Betts scores 26 but the Bruins fall 85-51 to UConn in the national semifinals — the largest margin in a women's semifinal ever.
UCLA enters the Elite Eight at 34-1, seeking the program's first NCAA championship.

subsections.title: Duke's Tournament History
subsections.content:
Duke has appeared in 28 NCAA Tournaments with a 66-27 record. The Blue Devils reached four Final Fours, all under coach Gail Goestenkors (1999, 2002, 2003, 2006), and appeared in two national championship games — losing to Purdue in 1999 and Tennessee in 2006. Under Kara Lawson, Duke has rebuilt into an ACC power, winning back-to-back conference tournament championships in 2025 and 2026.

This is Duke's first Elite Eight since Lawson took over — and their first since 2006. The Blue Devils haven't been to a Final Four in 20 years. A win over UCLA would end that drought and cement Lawson's program-building legacy.
